questionText: The lifetimes, $X$ hours, of a brand of light bulb are modelled by $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Testing shows that $10\%$ of bulbs fail before $800$ hours and $75\%$ fail before $1200$ hours. (a) Find $\mu$ and $\sigma$ to $3$ significant figures. (b) A shop sells a box of $500$ of these bulbs. Estimate how many will last more than $1000$ hours.

steps.description: State the model and the two given facts
steps.explanation: Lifetimes are normal with unknown $\mu$ and $\sigma$. ‘$10\%$ fail before $800$’ means $P(X<800)=0.10$ and ‘$75\%$ fail before $1200$’ means $P(X<1200)=0.75$. Both are lower-tail proportions, ready to standardise.
Standardising the lifetime $800$ with $Z=\dfrac{X-\mu}{\sigma}$ keeps the lower-tail area at $0.10$.
A point with only $10\%$ of the area below it lies well to the left of the mean, so the standardised value is negative.
Equating the standardised value of $800$ with $z_1$ gives the first equation in the unknowns.
The second statement is already lower-tail, so we standardise $1200$ and read off $z_2$. Because $75\%>50\%$, this $z$ is positive, sitting to the right of the mean.
Equating the standardised value of $1200$ with $z_2$ gives the second simultaneous equation.
Writing both as ‘value $-\ \mu = z\sigma$’ and subtracting removes $\mu$; the $400$-hour gap corresponds to about $1.96$ standard deviations.
Dividing the lifetime gap by the standardised gap gives a standard deviation of about $204$ hours.
From the first equation $\mu = 800 + 1.2816\,\sigma$. The mean lifetime of about $1060$ hours sits above $800$, consistent with only $10\%$ failing that early.
To three significant figures the fitted model is $X \sim N(1060,\ 204^2)$ hours, answering part (a).

steps.description: Set up part (b): probability a bulb lasts beyond 1000 hours
steps.explanation: To estimate how many bulbs exceed $1000$ hours we first find the probability for a single bulb, standardising $1000$ with the parameters just found.
Since $1000$ is a little below the mean of $1060$, its standardised value is slightly negative.
The area to the right of a point just left of the mean is a bit more than a half, giving about $0.616$. By symmetry $P(Z>-0.294)=\Phi(0.294)$.

steps.description: Scale up to the box of 500
steps.explanation: For $500$ independent bulbs the expected number lasting beyond $1000$ hours is the sample size multiplied by the single-bulb probability.
Rounding to a whole number, about $308$ of the $500$ bulbs are expected to last more than $1000$ hours.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: (a) $\mu \approx 1060$ hours, $\sigma \approx 204$ hours. (b) about $308$ of the $500$ bulbs.
